About¶

This notebook contains the main analytical workflow for a university research project on the economic feasibility of Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) systems in Hungary.

Project motivation¶

Hungary is experiencing rapid growth in renewable electricity production, especially from solar PV. This creates structural imbalances in the electricity system, including low midday prices, higher evening prices, and rising balancing needs. V2G may help address these patterns by allowing EV batteries to act as distributed storage.

Research objectives¶

The notebook develops a data-driven microeconomic assessment of V2G in Hungary. The main objectives are:

  • estimate arbitrage potential from charging at low-price hours and discharging at high-price hours,
  • quantify grid-side annual income, cost, and profit,
  • evaluate projected EV fleet growth and projected infrastructure costs,
  • forecast electricity prices through 2030,
  • assess 10-year financial feasibility, including incentives and NPV.

Workflow¶

The analysis is organized into the following stages:

  1. data ingestion from electricity-market, EV, battery-capacity, and infrastructure-cost datasets,
  2. cleaning and harmonization of modeling inputs,
  3. exploratory analysis of electricity-price dynamics,
  4. forecasting of EV stock, electricity prices, and infrastructure costs,
  5. simplified V2G economic modeling.

Output management¶

The notebook now uses an output folder:

../output/

Selected charts are saved there as PNG files and selected reporting tables are saved there as CSV files in addition to being shown in the notebook.

  • Shayan Ghiaseddin: MSc Business Informatics – Corvinus University of Budapest
  • Sofia Nikolaeva: MSc Business Informatics – Corvinus University of Budapest
  • Nurzada Nasirova: MSc Business Informatics – Corvinus University of Budapest

This is group project for Data Science project in Business course by Tibor Kovács, at Corvinus University of Budapest, Spring 2026

Packages and Libraries¶

# !{sys.executable} -m pip install -v pytimetk

In [40]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import re
import calendar
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.dates as mdates
from matplotlib.colors import LinearSegmentedColormap
from scipy.optimize import curve_fit
import pytimetk as tk
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor

Data Ingestion¶

In [41]:
path = '../data/'
path_output = '../output/'

def save_plot(filename, dpi=300):
    plt.savefig(path_output + f'{filename}.png', dpi=dpi, bbox_inches='tight')

def save_table(df, filename, index=False):
    df.to_csv(path_output + f'{filename}.csv', index=index)

# Electricity Price in Hungary
# ref: https://ember-energy.org/data/european-wholesale-electricity-price-data/
filename = 'ember-energy.org-electricity-price-hungary-20260308.csv'
price_df = pd.read_csv(path + filename)


# Count of Electric Vehicles (EV) in Hungary
# ref: https://alternative-fuels-observatory.ec.europa.eu/transport-mode/road/hungary
filename = 'alternative-fuels-observatory.ec.europa.eu-number-of-ev-hungary-20260308.csv'
ev_count_df = pd.read_csv(path + filename)


# Top selling EVs in Hungary
# ref: https://alternative-fuels-observatory.ec.europa.eu/transport-mode/road/hungary
filename = 'alternative-fuels-observatory.ec.europa.eu-best-selling-ev-hungary-20260308.csv'
ev_type_df = pd.read_csv(path + filename)


# Useable battery capacity of EVs
# ref: https://ev-database.org/cheatsheet/useable-battery-capacity-electric-car
filename = 'ev-database.org-ev-battery-capacity-20260308.csv'
battery_capacity_df = pd.read_csv(path + filename)


# Cost of converting common station into bidirectional station
# ref: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apenergy.2024.123679 --> Table 3
# note: this study has done in Germany
filename = 'Prospects of electric vehicle V2G multi-use_table3.csv'
station_conversion_cost_df = pd.read_csv(path + filename)

This dataset is already clean, but it would be better to

  • drop unnessecary columns: Country, ISO3 Code, Datetime (UTC)
  • change type "Datetime (Local)" into date-time
  • change price unit into EUR/KWh
In [44]:
# Keep only the columns we need
price_df = price_df.drop(columns=['Country', 'ISO3 Code', 'Datetime (UTC)'])

# Convert local datetime column to pandas datetime
price_df['Datetime'] = pd.to_datetime(price_df['Datetime (Local)'])

# Convert price from EUR/MWhe to EUR/kWh
# 1 MWh = 1000 kWh
price_df['Price'] = price_df['Price (EUR/MWhe)'] / 1000

# Drop old columns
price_df = price_df.drop(columns=['Datetime (Local)'])
price_df = price_df.drop(columns=['Price (EUR/MWhe)'])

This dataset is already clean, but it would be better to

In [48]:
# Keep only the columns we need
ev_count_df = ev_count_df.drop(columns=['H2', 'LPG', 'CNG', 'LNG'])

# Convert BEV and PHEV to int
ev_count_df['BEV'] = ev_count_df['BEV'].fillna(0).astype(int)
ev_count_df['PHEV'] = ev_count_df['PHEV'].fillna(0).astype(int)

# Add column EV = BEV + PHEV
ev_count_df['EV'] = ev_count_df['BEV'] + ev_count_df['PHEV']

# Rename Category into Year
ev_count_df = ev_count_df.rename(columns={'Category': 'Year'})

# Rename columns
ev_type_df = ev_type_df.rename(columns={
    'Category': 'EV Model',
    '2025': 'Unit Sold 2025'
})

# Remove 'BEV' from model names
ev_type_df['EV Model'] = ev_type_df['EV Model'].str.replace(' BEV', '', regex=False)

# replace 'BYD SEALION 07' with 'BYD SEALION7' 
ev_type_df['EV Model'] = ev_type_df['EV Model'].replace({
    'BYD SEALION 07': 'BYD SEALION7'
})

This dataset is already clean. Let's check if we can find capacity for top 10 EV Models...

In [56]:
# Create normalized copies for matching
def normalize_model_name(text):
    text = str(text).lower().strip()
    text = re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', text)
    return text

# Normalized copies
ev_type_tmp = ev_type_df.copy()
battery_tmp = battery_capacity_df.copy()

ev_type_tmp['model_key'] = ev_type_tmp['EV Model'].apply(normalize_model_name)
battery_tmp['model_key'] = battery_tmp['EV Model'].apply(normalize_model_name)

# Manual standardization
ev_type_tmp['model_key'] = ev_type_tmp['model_key'].replace({
    'byd sealion7': 'byd sealion 7'
})

battery_tmp['model_key'] = battery_tmp['model_key'].replace({
    'byd sealion7': 'byd sealion 7'
})

def find_matches(ev_key, battery_series):
    # Match as a phrase, not as a raw substring
    pattern = rf'(^|[^a-z0-9]){re.escape(ev_key)}([^a-z0-9]|$)'
    return battery_series.str.contains(pattern, regex=True, na=False)
    
# find all battery dataset rows where the battery model contains the top model
matches = []
for _, ev_row in ev_type_tmp.iterrows():
    ev_model = ev_row['EV Model']
    units_sold = ev_row['Unit Sold 2025']
    ev_key = ev_row['model_key']
    
    mask = find_matches(ev_key, battery_tmp['model_key'])
    matched_rows = battery_tmp[mask].copy()
    
    if matched_rows.empty:
        matches.append({
            'EV Model': ev_model,
            'Unit Sold 2025': units_sold,
            'Matched Battery Model': None,
            'Capacity': None
        })
    else:
        for _, bat_row in matched_rows.iterrows():
            matches.append({
                'EV Model': ev_model,
                'Unit Sold 2025': units_sold,
                'Matched Battery Model': bat_row['EV Model'],
                'Capacity': bat_row['Capacity']
            })

ev_type_matches_df = pd.DataFrame(matches)
ev_type_matches_df
Out[56]:

In [57]:
# summarize to one capacity per top-selling model

ev_profiles_df = (
    ev_type_matches_df
    .groupby(['EV Model', 'Unit Sold 2025'], as_index=False)
    .agg(
        Matched_Models=('Matched Battery Model', lambda x: ', '.join(x.dropna().astype(str))),
        Battery_Capacity=('Capacity', 'mean')
    )
)

# manualy add battery_capacity for 'BYD YUAN PLUS / ATTO-3' = average (49.92 kWh and 60.48 kWh)
ev_profiles_df.loc[
    ev_profiles_df['EV Model'] == 'BYD YUAN PLUS / ATTO-3',
    'Battery_Capacity'
] = (49.92 + 60.48) / 2

save_table(ev_profiles_df, 'report_ev_profiles_top10', index=False)
ev_profiles_df

Exploratory Data Analysis¶

Electricity Price¶

Here we want to see the fluctuations during 24 hours.

  • First we partion data and focus on year 2021.
  • Then, we will calculate averages over the months.
  • Finally we show the workdays vs. weekends
In [60]:
# Filter data for 2021
price_2021_df = price_df[
    price_df['Datetime'].dt.year == 2021
].copy()

price_2021_df['hour'] = price_2021_df['Datetime'].dt.hour
price_2021_df['month'] = price_2021_df['Datetime'].dt.month
price_2021_df['weekday'] = price_2021_df['Datetime'].dt.weekday

price_2021_df.head()
Out[60]:
Datetime Price hour month weekday
52607 2021-01-01 00:00:00 0.04554 0 1 4
52608 2021-01-01 01:00:00 0.04159 1 1 4
52609 2021-01-01 02:00:00 0.04005 2 1 4
52610 2021-01-01 03:00:00 0.03690 3 1 4
52611 2021-01-01 04:00:00 0.03447 4 1 4
In [61]:
# Average price by hour (24h profile)
hourly_avg_df = (
    price_2021_df
    .groupby('hour')['Price']
    .mean()
    .reset_index()
)

plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
plt.plot(hourly_avg_df['hour'], hourly_avg_df['Price'], marker='o')

# Add constant average line
avg_price = hourly_avg_df['Price'].mean()
plt.axhline(avg_price, linestyle='--', color='red', label=f'Overall Average ({avg_price:.4f})')

plt.fill_between(hourly_avg_df['hour'],
                 hourly_avg_df['Price'],
                 avg_price,
                 where=(hourly_avg_df['Price'] < avg_price),
                 alpha=0.2,
                 color='green',
                 label='Charging Opportunity')

plt.fill_between(hourly_avg_df['hour'],
                 hourly_avg_df['Price'],
                 avg_price,
                 where=(hourly_avg_df['Price'] > avg_price),
                 alpha=0.3,
                 color='red',
                 label='Discharging Opportunity')


plt.title('Average Electricity Price by Hour (Hungary, 2021)')
plt.xlabel('Hour of Day')
plt.ylabel('Price (EUR/kWh)')
plt.xticks(range(24))
plt.grid(True)
plt.legend()

save_plot('eda_hourly_avg_profile_2021')
save_table(hourly_avg_df, 'eda_hourly_avg_profile_2021', index=False)
plt.show()

Significant difference between hours 4 to 9 PM, and 0 to 5 AM

Drill down to specific dates:

In [62]:
def plot_price_window_with_daily_average(price_df, start_date, end_date, save_name=None):
    """
    Plot hourly electricity prices between start_date and end_date,
    with a daily average line shown separately for each day.

    Parameters
    ----------
    price_df : pd.DataFrame
        Must contain:
        - 'Datetime' as datetime64
        - 'Price' as numeric
    start_date : str or datetime-like
        Example: '2021-01-01'
    end_date : str or datetime-like
        Example: '2021-01-07'
    figsize : tuple
        Matplotlib figure size
    """
    df = price_df.copy()
    start_date = pd.to_datetime(start_date)
    end_date = pd.to_datetime(end_date)
    end_datetime = end_date + pd.Timedelta(days=1) - pd.Timedelta(hours=1)

    df = df[
        (df['Datetime'] >= start_date) &
        (df['Datetime'] <= end_datetime)
    ].copy()

    if df.empty:
        print('No data available in the selected date range.')
        return

    df = df.sort_values('Datetime').copy()
    df['date'] = df['Datetime'].dt.date
    df['daily_avg_price'] = df.groupby('date')['Price'].transform('mean')

    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(16, 6))

    ax.plot(df['Datetime'], df['Price'], marker='o', linewidth=1.5, label='Hourly Price')

    for _, day_df in df.groupby('date'):
        ax.plot(
            day_df['Datetime'],
            day_df['daily_avg_price'],
            linestyle='--',
            linewidth=2,
            color='red'
        )

    ax.fill_between(
        df['Datetime'],
        df['Price'],
        df['daily_avg_price'],
        where=(df['Price'] < df['daily_avg_price']),
        alpha=0.2,
        color='green',
        label='Charging Opportunity'
    )

    ax.fill_between(
        df['Datetime'],
        df['Price'],
        df['daily_avg_price'],
        where=(df['Price'] > df['daily_avg_price']),
        alpha=0.3,
        color='red',
        label='Discharging Opportunity'
    )

    ax.plot([], [], linestyle='--', linewidth=2, color='red', label='Daily Average')

    ax.set_title(f'Electricity Price Profile from {start_date.date()} to {end_date.date()}')
    ax.set_xlabel('Datetime')
    ax.set_ylabel('Price (EUR/kWh)')
    ax.grid(True)

    ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.DayLocator())
    ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%Y-%m-%d\n%H:%M'))

    plt.xticks(rotation=45)
    ax.legend()
    plt.tight_layout()
    if save_name is not None:
        save_plot(save_name)
        
    plt.show()
In [63]:
plot_price_window_with_daily_average(price_df, '2021-02-01', '2021-02-03')

Now let's see the same graphs on different months:

In [64]:
# Monthly hourly averages
monthly_hourly_df = (
    price_2021_df
    .groupby(['month','hour'])['Price']
    .mean()
    .reset_index()
)

# Pivot for easier plotting
monthly_pivot = monthly_hourly_df.pivot(
    index='hour',
    columns='month',
    values='Price'
)

monthly_pivot = monthly_pivot.sort_index(axis=1)

season_cmap = LinearSegmentedColormap.from_list(
    'season_cmap',
    ['blue', 'green', 'red', 'yellow'],
    N=12
)

colors = season_cmap(np.linspace(0, 1, 12))

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))

for i, month in enumerate(monthly_pivot.columns):
    plt.plot(
        monthly_pivot.index,
        monthly_pivot[month],
        label=calendar.month_name[month],
        color=colors[i]
    )

plt.title('Average Hourly Electricity Price by Month (2021)')
plt.xlabel('Hour')
plt.ylabel('Price (EUR/kWh)')
plt.xticks(range(24))
plt.legend(ncol=3)
plt.grid(True)

save_plot('eda_monthly_hourly_averages_2021')
plt.show()
save_table(monthly_hourly_df, 'eda_monthly_hourly_averages_2021', index=False)

During October, November, and December the difference between early evening prices with midnight are the highest!

Consequence: We have daily data and it is better to not aggregate it.

In [65]:
# Workdays vs Weekends
price_2021_df['is_weekend'] = price_2021_df['weekday'] >= 5

# Calculate hourly averages
weekday_weekend_df = (
    price_2021_df
    .groupby(['is_weekend','hour'])['Price']
    .mean()
    .reset_index()
)

# Split for plotting
weekday_df = weekday_weekend_df[weekday_weekend_df['is_weekend'] == False]
weekend_df = weekday_weekend_df[weekday_weekend_df['is_weekend'] == True]

plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))

plt.plot(weekday_df['hour'], weekday_df['Price'], label='Workdays')
plt.plot(weekend_df['hour'], weekend_df['Price'], label='Weekends')

plt.title('Electricity Price: Workdays vs Weekends (2021)')
plt.xlabel('Hour')
plt.ylabel('Price (EUR/kWh)')
plt.xticks(range(24))
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True)

save_plot('eda_workdays_vs_weekends_2021')
plt.show()
save_table(weekday_weekend_df, 'eda_workdays_vs_weekends_2021', index=False)

No matter workday or weekend, the same pattern between early evening and midnight is visible.

Consequence: We don't need to partition our data based on weekday.

Modeling¶

We start by partitioning data for year 2021, which we have essential data available.

Later we will extend our model to 2030, by timeseries prediction.

Assumptions¶

  • Consumer price is equal to generation, transmission and distribution value of Electricity. This is very big statement, but let's assume it.
  • The top 10 units sold could be used to calculate the average battery capacity.
  • BEV and PHEV are all EV.
  • No technical barriers for EV about the duration and pace of discharge and charge phases. However we assume linear discharge and charge.
  • Recharge after midnight fully restores the battery before morning departure.
  • Conversion from common charging station into V2G station is the only cost of this project.
  • Consider 'mid2021' in the station_conversion_cost_df in this model.

Parameters:¶

  • ev_param_v2g_compatible{ev_station_compatible, preferance}: The percentage of EV owners available V2G charging station at home, EV compatible with V2G, and prefer to participate in V2G
  • ev_param_v2g_available{workday, weekend}: The percentage of EV owners pluged-in to station in workday and weekend during early evening hours
  • ev_param_plugin_time: Based on EV owner behaviour, the time in early evening the EV could be pluged-in to station.
  • ev_param_plugin_battery_percentage: The percentage of battery at the moment of pluging-in.
  • ev_param_plugout_time: Based on EV owner behaviour, the time in the morning the EV could be pluged-out from station.
  • ev_param_safe_capacity: The minimum capacity of battery to not discharge into grid.
  • spread_threshold: The minimum acceptable spread to take part in V2G on a cycle
  • amortization_years: Amortization of investment. Default must assume 0.

Objective¶

  • The difference between discharge in early evening and charge during midnight must be calculated. It is interpreted as income for the grid.
  • The profit must be calculated by income - cost
In [66]:
ev_param_v2g_compatible = {
    'ev_station_compatible': 0.9, 
    'preferance': 0.9 
}

ev_param_v2g_available = {
    'workday': 0.9, 
    'weekend': 0.5
}

ev_param_plugin_time = 18      # early evening
ev_param_plugout_time = 7      # morning
ev_param_plugin_battery_percentage = 0.8
ev_param_safe_capacity = 0.3334
spread_threshold = 0.01        # 1 cent Euro
amortization_years = 10

Calculations¶

Prepare EV stock and battery inputs for 2021¶

In [67]:
# EV stock in 2021
ev_2021 = ev_count_df.loc[ev_count_df['Year'] == 2021, 'EV'].iloc[0]

# Average battery capacity from top 10 best-selling EVs
weighted_avg_battery_capacity_kwh = (ev_profiles_df['Unit Sold 2025'] * ev_profiles_df['Battery_Capacity']).sum() / ev_profiles_df['Unit Sold 2025'].sum()

print('EV stock in 2021:', ev_2021)
print('Weighted average battery capacity (kWh):', round(weighted_avg_battery_capacity_kwh, 2))

EV stock in 2021: 28301
Weighted average battery capacity (kWh): 70.75

Prepare station conversion cost¶

In [68]:
# Separate capex and annual opex
annual_metering_cost = station_conversion_cost_df.loc[
    station_conversion_cost_df['cost'] == 'Metering equipment',
    'mid2021'
].iloc[0]

capex_per_station = station_conversion_cost_df.loc[
    station_conversion_cost_df['cost'] != 'Metering equipment',
    'mid2021'
].sum()

annualized_capex_per_station = capex_per_station / amortization_years
annual_cost_per_station = annualized_capex_per_station + annual_metering_cost

print('CAPEX per station (EUR):', capex_per_station)
print('Annual metering cost per station (EUR/year):', annual_metering_cost)
print('Annualized CAPEX per station (EUR/year):', round(annualized_capex_per_station, 2))
print('Annual total cost per station (EUR/year):', round(annual_cost_per_station, 2))

CAPEX per station (EUR): 4350
Annual metering cost per station (EUR/year): 30
Annualized CAPEX per station (EUR/year): 435.0
Annual total cost per station (EUR/year): 465.0

Model-compatible EV pool¶

In [69]:
v2g_compatibility_rate = (
    ev_param_v2g_compatible['ev_station_compatible']
    * ev_param_v2g_compatible['preferance']
)

eligible_ev_2021 = ev_2021 * v2g_compatibility_rate

print('Compatibility rate:', round(v2g_compatibility_rate, 4))
print('Eligible EVs in 2021:', round(eligible_ev_2021))

Compatibility rate: 0.81
Eligible EVs in 2021: 22924

Build daily price features for 2021¶

In [70]:
price_2021_df = price_df[price_df['Datetime'].dt.year == 2021].copy()

price_2021_df['date'] = price_2021_df['Datetime'].dt.date
price_2021_df['hour'] = price_2021_df['Datetime'].dt.hour
price_2021_df['weekday'] = price_2021_df['Datetime'].dt.weekday
price_2021_df['day_type'] = np.where(price_2021_df['weekday'] < 5, 'workday', 'weekend')

Extract daily charge and discharge prices¶

In [71]:
daily_rows = []

for current_date, day_df in price_2021_df.groupby('date'):
    day_df = day_df.sort_values('hour').copy()

    # Daily average price over all 24 hours
    daily_avg_price = day_df['Price'].mean()

    # Discharge window: 18:00 to 00:00
    # We consider hours 18, 19, 20, 21, 22, 23
    # Keep only hours with price above that day's average
    evening_df = day_df[
        (day_df['hour'] >= ev_param_plugin_time) &
        (day_df['hour'] < 24)
    ].copy()

    discharge_hours_df = evening_df[
        evening_df['Price'] > daily_avg_price
    ].copy()

    if discharge_hours_df.empty:
        avg_discharge_price = np.nan
        discharge_hours_count = 0
    else:
        avg_discharge_price = discharge_hours_df['Price'].mean()
        discharge_hours_count = len(discharge_hours_df)

    # Charge window: 22:00 to 07:00 next morning
    # We approximate this as:
    # same date hours 22, 23
    # plus next date hours 0..6
    # -----------------------------
    next_date = current_date + pd.Timedelta(days=1)

    # Next day daily average price over all 24 hours
    next_day_avg_price = price_2021_df[
        price_2021_df['date'] == next_date
    ]['Price'].mean()

    same_night_df = price_2021_df[
        (price_2021_df['date'] == current_date) &
        (price_2021_df['hour'] >= 22)
    ][['Datetime', 'date', 'hour', 'Price']].copy()

    next_morning_df = price_2021_df[
        (price_2021_df['date'] == next_date) &
        (price_2021_df['hour'] < ev_param_plugout_time)
    ][['Datetime', 'date', 'hour', 'Price']].copy()

    charge_window_df = pd.concat([same_night_df, next_morning_df], ignore_index=True)

    # Only charge when price is not above the NEXT day's average
    charge_hours_df = charge_window_df[
        charge_window_df['Price'] <= next_day_avg_price
    ].copy()

    if charge_hours_df.empty:
        avg_charge_price = np.nan
        charge_hours_count = 0
    else:
        avg_charge_price = charge_hours_df['Price'].mean()
        charge_hours_count = len(charge_hours_df)

    spread = avg_discharge_price - avg_charge_price
    if ( spread < spread_threshold ):
        spread = 0

    daily_rows.append({
        'date': current_date,
        'day_type': 'workday' if pd.Timestamp(current_date).weekday() < 5 else 'weekend',
        'daily_avg_price': daily_avg_price,
        'avg_discharge_price': avg_discharge_price,
        'discharge_hours_count': discharge_hours_count,
        'avg_charge_price': avg_charge_price,
        'charge_hours_count': charge_hours_count,
        'spread': spread
    })

daily_price_df = pd.DataFrame(daily_rows)

# Dynamic spread
# daily_price_df['spread'] = (
#     daily_price_df['avg_discharge_price'] - daily_price_df['avg_charge_price']
# )

daily_price_df.head(10)
Out[71]:
date day_type daily_avg_price avg_discharge_price discharge_hours_count avg_charge_price charge_hours_count spread
0 2021-01-01 workday 0.039884 0.052387 6 0.041171 9 0.011216
1 2021-01-02 weekend 0.051420 0.058878 5 0.024615 6 0.034263
2 2021-01-03 weekend 0.038683 0.048002 5 0.029621 9 0.018381
3 2021-01-04 workday 0.051449 0.062310 4 0.035941 9 0.026369
4 2021-01-05 workday 0.052316 0.060337 3 0.038157 9 0.022180
5 2021-01-06 workday 0.052068 0.059257 4 0.038708 9 0.020550
6 2021-01-07 workday 0.065040 0.081333 3 0.049781 9 0.031552
7 2021-01-08 workday 0.074560 0.088285 2 0.034289 8 0.053996
8 2021-01-09 weekend 0.054987 0.066988 5 0.035680 7 0.031308
9 2021-01-10 weekend 0.047052 0.055298 5 0.034322 9 0.020976

Charge, discharge, and daily average for the Feb 2021

In [72]:
# Feb 2021 data
sample_df = daily_price_df[
    (daily_price_df['date'] >= pd.to_datetime('2021-02-01').date()) &
    (daily_price_df['date'] <= pd.to_datetime('2021-02-28').date())
].copy()
In [73]:
fig, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))

# Lines for prices
ax1.plot(sample_df['date'], sample_df['avg_discharge_price'], marker='o', label='Discharge Price')
ax1.plot(sample_df['date'], sample_df['avg_charge_price'], marker='o', label='Charge Price')
ax1.plot(sample_df['date'], sample_df['daily_avg_price'], marker='o', linestyle='--', label='Daily Average Price')

ax1.set_xlabel('Date')
ax1.set_ylabel('Price (EUR/kWh)')
ax1.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)
ax1.grid(True)

# Bar colors: weekend = red, workday = blue
bar_colors = sample_df['day_type'].map({
    'workday': 'blue',
    'weekend': 'red'
})

# Bars for spread
ax2 = ax1.twinx()
ax2.bar(sample_df['date'], sample_df['spread'], alpha=0.3, color=bar_colors, label='Spread')
ax2.set_ylabel('Spread (EUR/kWh)')

# Combined legend
lines1, labels1 = ax1.get_legend_handles_labels()

from matplotlib.patches import Patch
bar_legend = [
    Patch(facecolor='blue', alpha=0.3, label='Spread (Workday)'),
    Patch(facecolor='red', alpha=0.3, label='Spread (Weekend)')
]

ax1.legend(lines1 + bar_legend, labels1 + ['Spread (Workday)', 'Spread (Weekend)'], loc='upper left')

plt.title('Dynamic Charge / Discharge Prices and Spread - Feb 2021')
plt.tight_layout()
save_plot('model_dynamic_spread_sample')
plt.show()
save_table(sample_df, 'model_dynamic_spread_sample', index=False)

Investigate around Feb 20 to 22 to find out the anomality in the charge price and daily average:

In [74]:
plot_price_window_with_daily_average(price_df, '2021-02-20', '2021-02-22', 'electricity_price_profile_from_2021-02-20_to_22')

The spread is too little! The average price of evening hours in Feb 21 is almost equal to average price of midnight hours in the next day.

Let's fact check:

In [75]:
# Define the two periods
evening_start = pd.to_datetime('2021-02-21 16:00')
evening_end   = pd.to_datetime('2021-02-21 23:00')

night_start   = pd.to_datetime('2021-02-22 00:00')
night_end     = pd.to_datetime('2021-02-22 08:00')

# Evening prices (potential discharge)
evening_avg = price_df[
    (price_df['Datetime'] >= evening_start) &
    (price_df['Datetime'] <= evening_end)
]['Price'].mean()

# Night prices (charging)
night_avg = price_df[
    (price_df['Datetime'] >= night_start) &
    (price_df['Datetime'] <= night_end)
]['Price'].mean()

print(f"Average price Feb 21 18:00–23:00 : {evening_avg:.4f} EUR/kWh")
print(f"Average price Feb 22 00:00–05:00 : {night_avg:.4f} EUR/kWh")
print(f"Spread (evening - night)        : {(evening_avg-night_avg):.4f} EUR/kWh")

Average price Feb 21 18:00–23:00 : 0.0485 EUR/kWh
Average price Feb 22 00:00–05:00 : 0.0449 EUR/kWh
Spread (evening - night)        : 0.0036 EUR/kWh

Daily available EV count¶

In [76]:
daily_price_df['availability_rate'] = daily_price_df['day_type'].map(ev_param_v2g_available)

daily_price_df['available_ev_count'] = eligible_ev_2021 * daily_price_df['availability_rate']

daily_price_df.head()
Out[76]:
date day_type daily_avg_price avg_discharge_price discharge_hours_count avg_charge_price charge_hours_count spread availability_rate available_ev_count

1 2021-01-02 weekend 0.051420 0.058878 5 0.024615 6 0.034263 0.5 11461.905
2 2021-01-03 weekend 0.038683 0.048002 5 0.029621 9 0.018381 0.5 11461.905
3 2021-01-04 workday 0.051449 0.062310 4 0.035941 9 0.026369 0.9 20631.429
4 2021-01-05 workday 0.052316 0.060337 3 0.038157 9 0.022180 0.9 20631.429

Energy available per EV¶

In [77]:
usable_energy_per_ev_kwh = weighted_avg_battery_capacity_kwh * (ev_param_plugin_battery_percentage - ev_param_safe_capacity)

print('Usable energy per EV (kWh):', round(usable_energy_per_ev_kwh, 2))

Usable energy per EV (kWh): 33.01
In [78]:
daily_price_df['energy_to_grid_kwh'] = (
    daily_price_df['available_ev_count'] * usable_energy_per_ev_kwh
)

Daily income from arbitrage¶

In [79]:
# If spread is negative, this gives a loss. 
# Allow operation only when profitable:
daily_price_df['profitable_spread'] = daily_price_df['spread'].clip(lower=0)
daily_price_df['income_eur_profitable_only'] = (
    daily_price_df['energy_to_grid_kwh'] * daily_price_df['profitable_spread']
)
daily_price_df.head()
Out[79]:
date day_type daily_avg_price avg_discharge_price discharge_hours_count avg_charge_price charge_hours_count spread availability_rate available_ev_count energy_to_grid_kwh profitable_spread income_eur_profitable_only
0 2021-01-01 workday 0.039884 0.052387 6 0.041171 9 0.011216 0.9 20631.429 681124.424029 0.011216 7639.188818
1 2021-01-02 weekend 0.051420 0.058878 5 0.024615 6 0.034263 0.5 11461.905 378402.457794 0.034263 12965.203411
2 2021-01-03 weekend 0.038683 0.048002 5 0.029621 9 0.018381 0.5 11461.905 378402.457794 0.018381 6955.373532
3 2021-01-04 workday 0.051449 0.062310 4 0.035941 9 0.026369 0.9 20631.429 681124.424029 0.026369 17960.494257
4 2021-01-05 workday 0.052316 0.060337 3 0.038157 9 0.022180 0.9 20631.429 681124.424029 0.022180 15107.339725

Annual station cost¶

In [80]:
station_count = eligible_ev_2021
annual_cost_total = station_count * annual_cost_per_station

print('Estimated station count:', round(station_count))
print('Annual total cost (EUR/year):', round(annual_cost_total, 2))

Estimated station count: 22924
Annual total cost (EUR/year): 10659571.65

Annual profit¶

In [81]:
annual_income_eur = daily_price_df['income_eur_profitable_only'].sum()

annual_profit_eur = annual_income_eur - annual_cost_total

model_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    'Metric': [
        'EV stock 2021',
        'Eligible EVs',
        'Average battery capacity (kWh)',
        'Usable energy per EV (kWh)',
        'Station annual cost per unit (EUR/year)',
        'Total annual income (EUR)',
        'Total annual cost (EUR)',
        'Annual profit (EUR)',
        'Annual profit per EV (EUR)'
    ],
    'Value': [
        ev_2021,
        eligible_ev_2021,
        weighted_avg_battery_capacity_kwh,
        usable_energy_per_ev_kwh,
        annual_cost_per_station,
        annual_income_eur,
        annual_cost_total,
        annual_profit_eur,
        annual_profit_eur / eligible_ev_2021
    ]
})

model_summary['Value'] = model_summary['Value'].apply(
    lambda x: f"{x:,.0f}" if abs(x) >= 1 else f"{x:,.4f}"
)

display(model_summary)
save_table(model_summary, 'report_model_summary_2021', index=False)
Metric Value
0 EV stock 2021 28,301
1 Eligible EVs 22,924
2 Average battery capacity (kWh) 71
3 Usable energy per EV (kWh) 33
4 Station annual cost per unit (EUR/year) 465
5 Total annual income (EUR) 11,520,395
6 Total annual cost (EUR) 10,659,572
7 Annual profit (EUR) 860,823
8 Annual profit per EV (EUR) 38

Extend Data using Time Series Prediction¶

The idea is to run the model for a 10 years period from 2021 to 2030. This means we must project existing data into this period.

Existing time series:

  • price_df --> 2012/01 to 2026/02
  • ev_count_df --> 2008 to 2025
  • station_conversion_cost_df --> 2021 and 2030, not the years in between

Extend Station Conversion Cost¶

We just need to interpolate between 2021 → 2030 for each cost row and create values for every year.

In [82]:
cost_mid_df = station_conversion_cost_df[['cost', 'mid2021', 'mid2030']].copy()

# reshape to long format
cost_long_df = cost_mid_df.melt(
    id_vars='cost',
    value_vars=['mid2021', 'mid2030'],
    var_name='year',
    value_name='value'
)

cost_long_df['year'] = cost_long_df['year'].str.replace('mid', '').astype(int)

years = pd.DataFrame({'year': list(range(2021, 2031))})
costs = pd.DataFrame({'cost': cost_mid_df['cost'].unique()})

# create full year range for every cost type
full_grid_df = costs.merge(years, how='cross')

cost_interp_df = (
    full_grid_df
    .merge(cost_long_df, on=['cost', 'year'], how='left')
    .sort_values(['cost', 'year'])
)

# interpolate per cost type
cost_interp_df['value'] = (
    cost_interp_df
    .groupby('cost')['value']
    .transform(lambda s: s.interpolate(method='linear'))
)

# pivot back to wide
station_conversion_cost_yearly_df = cost_interp_df.pivot(
    index='cost',
    columns='year',
    values='value'
).reset_index()

save_table(station_conversion_cost_yearly_df, 'forecast_station_conversion_cost_2021_2030', index=False)
station_conversion_cost_yearly_df
Out[82]:
year cost 2021 2022 2023 2024 2025 2026 2027 2028 2029 2030
0 Additional hardware 450.0 411.111111 372.222222 333.333333 294.444444 255.555556 216.666667 177.777778 138.888889 100.0
1 EVSE installation 350.0 350.000000 350.000000 350.000000 350.000000 350.000000 350.000000 350.000000 350.000000 350.0
2 EVSE purchase 3550.0 3291.666667 3033.333333 2775.000000 2516.666667 2258.333333 2000.000000 1741.666667 1483.333333 1225.0
3 Metering equipment 30.0 30.000000 30.000000 30.000000 30.000000 30.000000 30.000000 30.000000 30.000000 30.0

Extend EV Count¶

The exiting data looks exponential. We need an exponential model with an upper cap.

In [83]:
# visualize the growth properly, on both normal scale and log scale.
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))

# Normal scale
axes[0].plot(ev_count_df['Year'], ev_count_df['EV'], marker='o')
axes[0].set_title('EV Count in Hungary (Normal Scale)')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Year')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Number of EV')
axes[0].grid(True)

# Log scale
axes[1].plot(ev_count_df['Year'], ev_count_df['EV'], marker='o')
axes[1].set_yscale('log')
axes[1].set_title('EV Count in Hungary (Log Scale)')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Year')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Number of EV (log)')
axes[1].grid(True)

plt.tight_layout()
save_plot('forecast_ev_count_normal_vs_log')
plt.show()

This looks roughly straight, then we can confirm exponential growth is a good model.

In [84]:
# Copy EV count data
ev_projection_df = ev_count_df.copy()

# Logistic growth function
def logistic_model(t, K, A, b):
    return K / (1 + A * np.exp(-b * t))

# Keep only positive EV counts for fitting
ev_fit_df = ev_projection_df[ev_projection_df['EV'] > 0].copy()

# Time index starting from first available year
base_year = ev_fit_df['Year'].min()
ev_fit_df['t'] = ev_fit_df['Year'] - base_year

# Prepare data for fitting
t_data = ev_fit_df['t'].values
y_data = ev_fit_df['EV'].values

# Initial parameter guesses
# K = upper cap, A and b are shape parameters
K0 = 1_000_000
A0 = 1000
b0 = 0.3

# Fit logistic model
params, _ = curve_fit(
    logistic_model,
    t_data,
    y_data,
    p0=[K0, A0, b0],
    maxfev=10000
)

K, A, b = params

print(f"Estimated K (upper cap): {K:,.0f}")
print(f"Estimated A: {A:.4f}")
print(f"Estimated b: {b:.4f}")

# Create projection until 2030
ev_projection_yearly_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Year': list(range(ev_projection_df['Year'].min(), 2031))
})

ev_projection_yearly_df['t'] = ev_projection_yearly_df['Year'] - base_year
ev_projection_yearly_df['EV_Projected'] = logistic_model(
    ev_projection_yearly_df['t'],
    K, A, b
)

# Round projected EV count
ev_projection_yearly_df['EV_Projected'] = ev_projection_yearly_df['EV_Projected'].round(0).astype(int)

# Merge actual values for comparison
ev_projection_yearly_df = ev_projection_yearly_df.merge(
    ev_count_df[['Year', 'EV']],
    on='Year',
    how='left'
)

# Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))

plt.plot(
    ev_projection_yearly_df['Year'],
    ev_projection_yearly_df['EV_Projected'],
    linestyle='--',
    marker='o',
    label='Logistic Projection'
)

plt.plot(
    ev_count_df['Year'],
    ev_count_df['EV'],
    linestyle='-',
    marker='o',
    label='Actual EV Count'
)

plt.title('EV Count Projection in Hungary (Logistic Growth with Upper Cap)')
plt.xlabel('Year')
plt.ylabel('Number of EV')
plt.grid(True)
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
save_plot('forecast_ev_count_logistic_projection')
plt.show()

display(ev_projection_yearly_df.tail(10))
save_table(ev_projection_yearly_df, 'forecast_ev_count_logistic_projection', index=False)

Estimated K (upper cap): 278,487
Estimated A: 1142.8235
Estimated b: 0.4763

In [85]:
# Create a df that use actual data for first 5 years, then use projected data for the second 5 years

ev_count_2021_2030_df = ev_projection_yearly_df[
    (ev_projection_yearly_df['Year'] >= 2021) &
    (ev_projection_yearly_df['Year'] <= 2030)
].copy()

ev_count_2021_2030_df['EV'] = ev_count_2021_2030_df['EV'].fillna(
    ev_count_2021_2030_df['EV_Projected']
)

ev_count_2021_2030_df['EV'] = ev_count_2021_2030_df['EV'].astype(int)

ev_count_2021_2030_df = ev_count_2021_2030_df[['Year', 'EV']]

save_table(ev_count_2021_2030_df, 'forecast_ev_count_2021_2030_final', index=False)
ev_count_2021_2030_df

21 2029 229033
22 2030 245557

Extend Electricity Price¶

For this, we need to use a proper time series prediction with seasonality considerations.

Let's use PyTimeTK: https://business-science.github.io/pytimetk/

In [86]:
# create time series
price_ts_df = price_df[price_df['Datetime'] >= '2020-01-01 00:00:00'].copy()
price_ts_df = price_ts_df.sort_values('Datetime')
price_ts_df = price_ts_df[['Datetime', 'Price']].copy()

# we have some duplicates in our dataset
duplicate_count = price_ts_df['Datetime'].duplicated().sum()
print('Duplicate Datetime rows:', duplicate_count)

price_ts_df[price_ts_df['Datetime'].duplicated(keep=False)].head(10)

Duplicate Datetime rows: 6
Out[86]:
Datetime Price
50976 2020-10-25 02:00:00 0.01869
50977 2020-10-25 02:00:00 0.01892
59880 2021-10-31 02:00:00 0.09510
59881 2021-10-31 02:00:00 0.08003
68616 2022-10-30 02:00:00 0.09992
68617 2022-10-30 02:00:00 0.09828
77352 2023-10-29 02:00:00 0.01216
77353 2023-10-29 02:00:00 0.01074
86088 2024-10-27 02:00:00 0.08223
86089 2024-10-27 02:00:00 0.08043
In [87]:
# aggregate duplicates into one value (average) per timestamp before asfreq
price_ts_df = (
    price_ts_df
    .groupby('Datetime', as_index=False)['Price']
    .mean()
    .sort_values('Datetime')
)

price_ts_df = price_ts_df.set_index('Datetime')
price_ts_df = price_ts_df.asfreq('h')

price_ts_df.head()
Out[87]:
Price
Datetime
2020-01-01 00:00:00 0.03355
2020-01-01 01:00:00 0.03040
2020-01-01 02:00:00 0.02853
2020-01-01 03:00:00 0.02737
2020-01-01 04:00:00 0.02638
In [88]:
# Check null values
print('Missing values before fill:', price_ts_df['Price'].isna().sum())

# Fill missing values using time interpolation
price_ts_df['Price'] = price_ts_df['Price'].interpolate(method='time')

# If any nulls remain at the edges, fill them forward/backward
price_ts_df['Price'] = price_ts_df['Price'].bfill().ffill()

# Check again
print('Missing values after fill:', price_ts_df['Price'].isna().sum())

price_ts_df.info()
price_ts_df.head()

Missing values before fill: 78
Missing values after fill: 0

DatetimeIndex: 53928 entries, 2020-01-01 00:00:00 to 2026-02-24 23:00:00
Freq: h
Data columns (total 1 columns):
 #   Column  Non-Null Count  Dtype  
---  ------  --------------  -----  
 0   Price   53928 non-null  float64
dtypes: float64(1)
memory usage: 842.6 KB
Out[88]:
Price
Datetime
2020-01-01 00:00:00 0.03355
2020-01-01 01:00:00 0.03040
2020-01-01 02:00:00 0.02853
2020-01-01 03:00:00 0.02737

Time Series Training¶

In [89]:
# We want forecast to last known point: 2030-12-31
last_date = price_ts_df.index.max()
forecast_end = pd.Timestamp('2030-12-31 23:00:00')
n_hours = int((forecast_end - last_date).total_seconds() / 3600)


# Forecast with PyTimeTK: We use seasonality aware model.
price_model_df = price_ts_df.copy().reset_index()

# Add time features
price_model_df = tk.augment_timeseries_signature(
    price_model_df,
    date_column='Datetime'
)
In [90]:
# Feature selection
features = [
    'Datetime_hour',
    'Datetime_wday',
    'Datetime_month',
    'Datetime_year',
    'Datetime_yweek',
    'Datetime_yday',
    'Datetime_weekend'
]

target = 'Price'
In [91]:
# Train with Random Forest
model = RandomForestRegressor(
    n_estimators=200,
    random_state=42,
    n_jobs=-1
)

model.fit(
    price_model_df[features],
    price_model_df[target]
)
Out[91]:
RandomForestRegressor(n_estimators=200, n_jobs=-1, random_state=42)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
Parameters
n_estimators n_estimators: int, default=100

The number of trees in the forest.

.. versionchanged:: 0.22
The default value of ``n_estimators`` changed from 10 to 100
in 0.22. 		200
criterion criterion: {"squared_error", "absolute_error", "friedman_mse", "poisson"}, default="squared_error"

The function to measure the quality of a split. Supported criteria
are "squared_error" for the mean squared error, which is equal to
variance reduction as feature selection criterion and minimizes the L2
loss using the mean of each terminal node, "friedman_mse", which uses
mean squared error with Friedman's improvement score for potential
splits, "absolute_error" for the mean absolute error, which minimizes
the L1 loss using the median of each terminal node, and "poisson" which
uses reduction in Poisson deviance to find splits.
Training using "absolute_error" is significantly slower
than when using "squared_error".

.. versionadded:: 0.18
Mean Absolute Error (MAE) criterion.

.. versionadded:: 1.0
Poisson criterion. 		'squared_error'
max_depth max_depth: int, default=None

The maximum depth of the tree. If None, then nodes are expanded until
all leaves are pure or until all leaves contain less than
min_samples_split samples. 		None
min_samples_split min_samples_split: int or float, default=2

The minimum number of samples required to split an internal node:

- If int, then consider `min_samples_split` as the minimum number.
- If float, then `min_samples_split` is a fraction and
`ceil(min_samples_split * n_samples)` are the minimum
number of samples for each split.

.. versionchanged:: 0.18
Added float values for fractions. 		2
min_samples_leaf min_samples_leaf: int or float, default=1

The minimum number of samples required to be at a leaf node.
A split point at any depth will only be considered if it leaves at
least ``min_samples_leaf`` training samples in each of the left and
right branches. This may have the effect of smoothing the model,
especially in regression.

- If int, then consider `min_samples_leaf` as the minimum number.
- If float, then `min_samples_leaf` is a fraction and
`ceil(min_samples_leaf * n_samples)` are the minimum
number of samples for each node.

.. versionchanged:: 0.18
Added float values for fractions. 		1
min_weight_fraction_leaf min_weight_fraction_leaf: float, default=0.0

The minimum weighted fraction of the sum total of weights (of all
the input samples) required to be at a leaf node. Samples have
equal weight when sample_weight is not provided. 		0.0
max_features max_features: {"sqrt", "log2", None}, int or float, default=1.0

The number of features to consider when looking for the best split:

- If int, then consider `max_features` features at each split.
- If float, then `max_features` is a fraction and
`max(1, int(max_features * n_features_in_))` features are considered at each
split.
- If "sqrt", then `max_features=sqrt(n_features)`.
- If "log2", then `max_features=log2(n_features)`.
- If None or 1.0, then `max_features=n_features`.

.. note::
The default of 1.0 is equivalent to bagged trees and more
randomness can be achieved by setting smaller values, e.g. 0.3.

.. versionchanged:: 1.1
The default of `max_features` changed from `"auto"` to 1.0.

Note: the search for a split does not stop until at least one
valid partition of the node samples is found, even if it requires to
effectively inspect more than ``max_features`` features. 		1.0
max_leaf_nodes max_leaf_nodes: int, default=None

Grow trees with ``max_leaf_nodes`` in best-first fashion.
Best nodes are defined as relative reduction in impurity.
If None then unlimited number of leaf nodes. 		None
min_impurity_decrease min_impurity_decrease: float, default=0.0

A node will be split if this split induces a decrease of the impurity
greater than or equal to this value.

The weighted impurity decrease equation is the following::

N_t / N * (impurity - N_t_R / N_t * right_impurity
- N_t_L / N_t * left_impurity)

where ``N`` is the total number of samples, ``N_t`` is the number of
samples at the current node, ``N_t_L`` is the number of samples in the
left child, and ``N_t_R`` is the number of samples in the right child.

``N``, ``N_t``, ``N_t_R`` and ``N_t_L`` all refer to the weighted sum,
if ``sample_weight`` is passed.

.. versionadded:: 0.19 		0.0
bootstrap bootstrap: bool, default=True

Whether bootstrap samples are used when building trees. If False, the
whole dataset is used to build each tree. 		True
oob_score oob_score: bool or callable, default=False

Whether to use out-of-bag samples to estimate the generalization score.
By default, :func:`~sklearn.metrics.r2_score` is used.
Provide a callable with signature `metric(y_true, y_pred)` to use a
custom metric. Only available if `bootstrap=True`.

For an illustration of out-of-bag (OOB) error estimation, see the example
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_ensemble_plot_ensemble_oob.py`. 		False
n_jobs n_jobs: int, default=None

The number of jobs to run in parallel. :meth:`fit`, :meth:`predict`,
:meth:`decision_path` and :meth:`apply` are all parallelized over the
trees. ``None`` means 1 unless in a :obj:`joblib.parallel_backend`
context. ``-1`` means using all processors. See :term:`Glossary
` for more details. 		-1
random_state random_state: int, RandomState instance or None, default=None

Controls both the randomness of the bootstrapping of the samples used
when building trees (if ``bootstrap=True``) and the sampling of the
features to consider when looking for the best split at each node
(if ``max_features < n_features``).
See :term:`Glossary ` for details. 		42
verbose verbose: int, default=0

Controls the verbosity when fitting and predicting. 		0
warm_start warm_start: bool, default=False

When set to ``True``, reuse the solution of the previous call to fit
and add more estimators to the ensemble, otherwise, just fit a whole
new forest. See :term:`Glossary ` and
:ref:`tree_ensemble_warm_start` for details. 		False
ccp_alpha ccp_alpha: non-negative float, default=0.0

Complexity parameter used for Minimal Cost-Complexity Pruning. The
subtree with the largest cost complexity that is smaller than
``ccp_alpha`` will be chosen. By default, no pruning is performed. See
:ref:`minimal_cost_complexity_pruning` for details. See
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_tree_plot_cost_complexity_pruning.py`
for an example of such pruning.

.. versionadded:: 0.22 		0.0
max_samples max_samples: int or float, default=None

If bootstrap is True, the number of samples to draw from X
to train each base estimator.

- If None (default), then draw `X.shape[0]` samples.
- If int, then draw `max_samples` samples.
- If float, then draw `max(round(n_samples * max_samples), 1)` samples. Thus,
`max_samples` should be in the interval `(0.0, 1.0]`.

.. versionadded:: 0.22 		None
monotonic_cst monotonic_cst: array-like of int of shape (n_features), default=None

Indicates the monotonicity constraint to enforce on each feature.
- 1: monotonically increasing
- 0: no constraint
- -1: monotonically decreasing

If monotonic_cst is None, no constraints are applied.

Monotonicity constraints are not supported for:
- multioutput regressions (i.e. when `n_outputs_ > 1`),
- regressions trained on data with missing values.

Read more in the :ref:`User Guide `.

.. versionadded:: 1.4 		None
In [92]:
# Create future dataframe
future_index = pd.date_range(
    start=last_date + pd.Timedelta(hours=1),
    end='2030-12-31 23:00:00',
    freq='h'
)

future_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Datetime': future_index
})

future_df = tk.augment_timeseries_signature(
    future_df,
    date_column='Datetime'
)

# Predict
future_df['Price_Predicted'] = model.predict(
    future_df[features]
)

Combine actual and forecast¶

In [93]:
actual_df = price_ts_df.reset_index()

actual_df['Price_Predicted'] = actual_df['Price']

price_forecast_df = pd.concat(
    [
        actual_df[['Datetime', 'Price_Predicted']],
        future_df[['Datetime', 'Price_Predicted']]
    ],
    ignore_index=True
)

save_table(price_forecast_df, 'forecast_electricity_price_2015_2030', index=False)
price_forecast_df
Out[93]:
Datetime Price_Predicted
0 2020-01-01 00:00:00 0.033550
1 2020-01-01 01:00:00 0.030400
2 2020-01-01 02:00:00 0.028530
3 2020-01-01 03:00:00 0.027370
4 2020-01-01 04:00:00 0.026380
... ... ...
96427 2030-12-31 19:00:00 0.119213
96428 2030-12-31 20:00:00 0.112931
96429 2030-12-31 21:00:00 0.106123
96430 2030-12-31 22:00:00 0.103845
96431 2030-12-31 23:00:00 0.092144

96432 rows × 2 columns

In [94]:
# Plot forecast

plt.figure(figsize=(12,5))

plt.plot(
    price_forecast_df['Datetime'],
    price_forecast_df['Price_Predicted'],
    linewidth=1
)

plt.title('Electricity price forecast to 2030')
plt.xlabel('Date')
plt.ylabel('Price EUR/kWh')
plt.grid(True)

save_plot('forecast_electricity_price_to_2030')
plt.show()

As we can see, this method keeps:

  • daily cycle
  • weekly cycle
  • yearly cycle
  • trend

But does not explode like exponential. This is a conservative chioce for feasibility simulation.

Modeling for 10 years: 2021 to 2030¶

In [95]:
# parameters
ev_param_v2g_compatible = {
    'ev_station_compatible': 0.8, 
    'preferance': 0.9 
}

ev_param_v2g_available = {
    'workday': 0.9, 
    'weekend': 0.5
}

ev_param_plugin_time = 18      # early evening
ev_param_plugout_time = 7      # morning
ev_param_plugin_battery_percentage = 0.8
ev_param_safe_capacity = 0.3334
amortization_years = 10
In [96]:
# Parameters and common inputs for the 10-year model
model_years = list(range(2021, 2031))

v2g_compatibility_rate = (
    ev_param_v2g_compatible['ev_station_compatible']
    * ev_param_v2g_compatible['preferance']
)

usable_energy_per_ev_kwh = weighted_avg_battery_capacity_kwh * (ev_param_plugin_battery_percentage - ev_param_safe_capacity)

price_model_2021_2030_df = (
    price_forecast_df
    .rename(columns={'Price_Predicted': 'Price'})
    .copy()
)

price_model_2021_2030_df['Datetime'] = pd.to_datetime(price_model_2021_2030_df['Datetime'])
price_model_2021_2030_df = price_model_2021_2030_df.sort_values('Datetime').reset_index(drop=True)

price_model_2021_2030_df = price_model_2021_2030_df[
    (price_model_2021_2030_df['Datetime'] >= pd.Timestamp('2021-01-01 00:00:00')) &
    (price_model_2021_2030_df['Datetime'] <= pd.Timestamp('2030-12-31 23:00:00'))
].copy()

price_model_2021_2030_df['date'] = price_model_2021_2030_df['Datetime'].dt.date
price_model_2021_2030_df['hour'] = price_model_2021_2030_df['Datetime'].dt.hour
price_model_2021_2030_df['weekday'] = price_model_2021_2030_df['Datetime'].dt.weekday
price_model_2021_2030_df['day_type'] = np.where(
    price_model_2021_2030_df['weekday'] < 5, 'workday', 'weekend'
)
In [97]:
# Helper and Model functions
def get_station_cost_components(year, station_cost_df, amortization_years):
    year_col = int(year)

    annual_metering_cost = station_cost_df.loc[
        station_cost_df['cost'] == 'Metering equipment',
        year_col
    ].iloc[0]

    capex_per_station = station_cost_df.loc[
        station_cost_df['cost'] != 'Metering equipment',
        year_col
    ].sum()

    annualized_capex_per_station = (
        0 if amortization_years == 0 else capex_per_station / amortization_years
    )

    annual_cost_per_station = annualized_capex_per_station + annual_metering_cost

    return {
        'annual_metering_cost': annual_metering_cost,
        'capex_per_station': capex_per_station,
        'annualized_capex_per_station': annualized_capex_per_station,
        'annual_cost_per_station': annual_cost_per_station
    }


def build_daily_price_profile_for_year(price_input_df, model_year, plugin_time, plugout_time):
    year_price_df = price_input_df[price_input_df['Datetime'].dt.year == model_year].copy()

    daily_rows = []

    for current_date, day_df in year_price_df.groupby('date'):
        day_df = day_df.sort_values('hour').copy()

        # Daily average price over all 24 hours of the current day
        daily_avg_price = day_df['Price'].mean()

        # Discharge window: plugin_time to 23:00
        discharge_hours_df = day_df[
            (day_df['hour'] >= plugin_time) &
            (day_df['hour'] <= 23) &
            (day_df['Price'] > daily_avg_price)
        ].copy()

        if discharge_hours_df.empty:
            avg_discharge_price = np.nan
            discharge_hours_count = 0
        else:
            avg_discharge_price = discharge_hours_df['Price'].mean()
            discharge_hours_count = len(discharge_hours_df)

        next_date = current_date + pd.Timedelta(days=1)

        # Next day daily average price over all 24 hours
        next_day_df = price_input_df[price_input_df['date'] == next_date].copy()
        next_day_avg_price = next_day_df['Price'].mean()

        same_night_df = price_input_df[
            (price_input_df['date'] == current_date) &
            (price_input_df['hour'] >= 22)
        ][['Datetime', 'date', 'hour', 'Price']].copy()

        next_morning_df = price_input_df[
            (price_input_df['date'] == next_date) &
            (price_input_df['hour'] < plugout_time)
        ][['Datetime', 'date', 'hour', 'Price']].copy()

        charge_window_df = pd.concat([same_night_df, next_morning_df], ignore_index=True)

        # Only charge when price is not above the NEXT day's average
        charge_hours_df = charge_window_df[
            charge_window_df['Price'] <= next_day_avg_price
        ].copy()

        if charge_hours_df.empty:
            avg_charge_price = np.nan
            charge_hours_count = 0
        else:
            avg_charge_price = charge_hours_df['Price'].mean()
            charge_hours_count = len(charge_hours_df)

        daily_rows.append({
            'date': current_date,
            'day_type': 'workday' if pd.Timestamp(current_date).weekday() < 5 else 'weekend',
            'daily_avg_price': daily_avg_price,
            'next_day_avg_price': next_day_avg_price,
            'avg_discharge_price': avg_discharge_price,
            'discharge_hours_count': discharge_hours_count,
            'avg_charge_price': avg_charge_price,
            'charge_hours_count': charge_hours_count
        })

    daily_price_profile_df = pd.DataFrame(daily_rows)
    daily_price_profile_df['spread'] = (
        daily_price_profile_df['avg_discharge_price'] - daily_price_profile_df['avg_charge_price']
    )
    daily_price_profile_df['profitable_spread'] = daily_price_profile_df['spread'].clip(lower=spread_threshold)

    return daily_price_profile_df


def run_v2g_model_for_year(
    model_year,
    ev_count_yearly_df,
    station_cost_yearly_df,
    price_input_df,
    v2g_compatibility_rate,
    ev_param_v2g_available,
    usable_energy_per_ev_kwh,
    plugin_time,
    plugout_time,
    amortization_years,
    previous_station_count=0,
    previous_annual_cost_total=0
):
    ev_stock = ev_count_yearly_df.loc[
        ev_count_yearly_df['Year'] == model_year, 'EV'
    ].iloc[0]

    eligible_ev = ev_stock * v2g_compatibility_rate

    station_costs = get_station_cost_components(
        year=model_year,
        station_cost_df=station_cost_yearly_df,
        amortization_years=amortization_years
    )

    daily_price_profile_df = build_daily_price_profile_for_year(
        price_input_df=price_input_df,
        model_year=model_year,
        plugin_time=plugin_time,
        plugout_time=plugout_time
    )

    daily_price_profile_df['availability_rate'] = daily_price_profile_df['day_type'].map(
        ev_param_v2g_available
    )

    daily_price_profile_df['available_ev_count'] = (
        eligible_ev * daily_price_profile_df['availability_rate']
    )

    daily_price_profile_df['energy_to_grid_kwh'] = (
        daily_price_profile_df['available_ev_count'] * usable_energy_per_ev_kwh
    )

    daily_price_profile_df['income_eur_profitable_only'] = (
        daily_price_profile_df['energy_to_grid_kwh'] * daily_price_profile_df['profitable_spread']
    )

    station_count = eligible_ev
    new_stations_added = max(station_count - previous_station_count, 0)
    incremental_annual_cost_eur = new_stations_added * station_costs['annual_cost_per_station']
    annual_cost_total = previous_annual_cost_total + incremental_annual_cost_eur

    annual_income_eur = daily_price_profile_df['income_eur_profitable_only'].sum()
    annual_profit_eur = annual_income_eur - annual_cost_total

    discharge_hours_count_sum = daily_price_profile_df['discharge_hours_count'].sum()
    if discharge_hours_count_sum > 0 and eligible_ev > 0:
        surplus_per_kwh = annual_profit_eur / (eligible_ev * usable_energy_per_ev_kwh * discharge_hours_count_sum)
    else:
        surplus_per_kwh = 0

    annual_summary = {
        'Year': model_year,
        'EV_Stock': ev_stock,
        'Eligible_EVs': eligible_ev,
        'Usable_Energy_per_EV_kWh': usable_energy_per_ev_kwh,
        'Average_Daily_Spread_EUR_per_kWh': daily_price_profile_df['profitable_spread'].mean(),
        'Average_Charge_Price_EUR_per_kWh': daily_price_profile_df['avg_charge_price'].mean(),
        'Average_Discharge_Price_EUR_per_kWh': daily_price_profile_df['avg_discharge_price'].mean(),
        'Average_Availability_Rate': daily_price_profile_df['availability_rate'].mean(),
        'Station_CAPEX_per_Unit_EUR': station_costs['capex_per_station'],
        'Annualized_CAPEX_per_Unit_EUR': station_costs['annualized_capex_per_station'],
        'Annual_Metering_per_Unit_EUR': station_costs['annual_metering_cost'],
        'Annual_Cost_per_Unit_EUR': station_costs['annual_cost_per_station'],
        'Previous_Station_Count': previous_station_count,
        'New_Stations_Added': new_stations_added,
        'Station_Count': station_count,
        'Incremental_Annual_Cost_EUR': incremental_annual_cost_eur,
        'Annual_Income_EUR': annual_income_eur,
        'Annual_Cost_EUR': annual_cost_total,
        'Annual_Profit_EUR': annual_profit_eur,
        'Annual_Profit_per_EV_EUR': annual_profit_eur / eligible_ev,
        'Surplus_per_kWh_EUR_Cent': surplus_per_kwh * 100
    }

    return annual_summary, daily_price_profile_df

Model Run¶

In [98]:
# Run the 10-year model
annual_results = []
daily_profile_frames = []

previous_station_count = 0
previous_annual_cost_total = 0

for model_year in model_years:
    annual_summary, daily_profile_year_df = run_v2g_model_for_year(
        model_year=model_year,
        ev_count_yearly_df=ev_count_2021_2030_df,
        station_cost_yearly_df=station_conversion_cost_yearly_df,
        price_input_df=price_model_2021_2030_df,
        v2g_compatibility_rate=v2g_compatibility_rate,
        ev_param_v2g_available=ev_param_v2g_available,
        usable_energy_per_ev_kwh=usable_energy_per_ev_kwh,
        plugin_time=ev_param_plugin_time,
        plugout_time=ev_param_plugout_time,
        amortization_years=amortization_years,
        previous_station_count=previous_station_count,
        previous_annual_cost_total=previous_annual_cost_total
    )

    annual_results.append(annual_summary)
    daily_profile_frames.append(daily_profile_year_df)

    previous_station_count = annual_summary['Station_Count']
    previous_annual_cost_total = annual_summary['Annual_Cost_EUR']

annual_model_2021_2030_df = pd.DataFrame(annual_results)
daily_model_2021_2030_df = pd.concat(daily_profile_frames, ignore_index=True)
In [99]:
# Readable annual summary
annual_model_2021_2030_display_df = annual_model_2021_2030_df.copy()

display_columns_0dp = [
    'EV_Stock', 'Eligible_EVs', 'Station_CAPEX_per_Unit_EUR',
    'Annualized_CAPEX_per_Unit_EUR', 'Annual_Metering_per_Unit_EUR',
    'Annual_Cost_per_Unit_EUR', 'Previous_Station_Count',
    'New_Stations_Added', 'Station_Count', 'Incremental_Annual_Cost_EUR',
    'Annual_Income_EUR', 'Annual_Cost_EUR', 'Annual_Profit_EUR', 'Annual_Profit_per_EV_EUR', 'Surplus_per_kWh_EUR_Cent'
]

display_columns_4dp = [
    'Usable_Energy_per_EV_kWh', 'Average_Daily_Spread_EUR_per_kWh',
    'Average_Charge_Price_EUR_per_kWh', 'Average_Discharge_Price_EUR_per_kWh',
    'Average_Availability_Rate'
]

for col in display_columns_0dp:
    annual_model_2021_2030_display_df[col] = annual_model_2021_2030_display_df[col].map(lambda x: f'{x:,.0f}')

for col in display_columns_4dp:
    annual_model_2021_2030_display_df[col] = annual_model_2021_2030_display_df[col].map(lambda x: f'{x:,.4f}')

10 rows × 21 columns

In [101]:
# NPV assumptions
discount_rate = 0.06   # 6% nominal discount rate
base_year = 2021

annual_model_2021_2030_df = annual_model_2021_2030_df.copy()

annual_model_2021_2030_df['Year_Index'] = (
    annual_model_2021_2030_df['Year'] - base_year
)

annual_model_2021_2030_df['Discount_Factor'] = (
    1 / (1 + discount_rate) ** annual_model_2021_2030_df['Year_Index']
)

annual_model_2021_2030_df['Discounted_Profit_EUR'] = (
    annual_model_2021_2030_df['Annual_Profit_EUR'] *
    annual_model_2021_2030_df['Discount_Factor']
)

annual_model_2021_2030_df[
    ['Year', 'Annual_Profit_EUR', 'Discount_Factor', 'Discounted_Profit_EUR']
]

npv_2021_2030 = annual_model_2021_2030_df['Discounted_Profit_EUR'].sum()

print(f"NPV (2021-2030) at {discount_rate:.0%} discount rate: {npv_2021_2030:,.0f} EUR")

NPV (2021-2030) at 6% discount rate: 126,567,695 EUR
In [102]:
# 10-year totals and a quick profit chart
model_10y_summary_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Metric': [
        'Total income, 2021-2030 (EUR)',
        'Total cost, 2021-2030 (EUR)',
        'Total profit, 2021-2030 (EUR)',
        'Average annual profit (EUR)',
        f'NPV, 2021-2030 @ {discount_rate:.0%} (EUR)'
    ],
    'Value': [
        annual_model_2021_2030_df['Annual_Income_EUR'].sum(),
        annual_model_2021_2030_df['Annual_Cost_EUR'].sum(),
        annual_model_2021_2030_df['Annual_Profit_EUR'].sum(),
        annual_model_2021_2030_df['Annual_Profit_EUR'].mean(),
        npv_2021_2030
    ]
})

model_10y_summary_df['Value'] = model_10y_summary_df['Value'].map(lambda x: f'{x:,.0f}')
save_table(model_10y_summary_df, 'model_summary_2021_2030', index=False)
display(model_10y_summary_df)

plt.figure(figsize=(9, 5))

plt.plot(
    annual_model_2021_2030_df['Year'],
    annual_model_2021_2030_df['Annual_Profit_EUR'],
    marker='o',
    label='Annual Profit'
)

plt.plot(
    annual_model_2021_2030_df['Year'],
    annual_model_2021_2030_df['Discounted_Profit_EUR'],
    marker='o',
    label='Discounted Profit'
)

plt.title(f'Annual Profit vs Discounted Profit ({discount_rate:.0%} discount rate)')
plt.xlabel('Year')
plt.ylabel('EUR')
plt.grid(True)
plt.legend()
save_plot('annual_profit_discount_profit')
plt.show()
Metric Value
0 Total income, 2021-2030 (EUR) 525,571,249
1 Total cost, 2021-2030 (EUR) 352,172,268
2 Total profit, 2021-2030 (EUR) 173,398,981
3 Average annual profit (EUR) 17,339,898
4 NPV, 2021-2030 @ 6% (EUR) 126,567,695

Incentives Calcualtion¶

Assuming the profit of this project must go back to EV owners, here we want to model the incentives. From the perspective of liberal economics, we think it would be better to share this profit by price surplus during the discharge. For instance, if the price on 19:00 is 5 cent, we want to propose 5.1 cent price to discharge the EV battery into grid. We assumed this would be the healthiest regime for the economic dynamics of this project.

We have calculated the average surplus for each year. We have distributed the annual profit across all discharge hours of all participating EVs.

In [103]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))

plt.plot(
    annual_model_2021_2030_df['Year'],
    annual_model_2021_2030_df['Surplus_per_kWh_EUR_Cent'] + annual_model_2021_2030_df['Average_Discharge_Price_EUR_per_kWh'] * 100,
    marker='o',
    label='Propesed Discharge Price for EV Owners'
)

plt.plot(
    annual_model_2021_2030_df['Year'],
    annual_model_2021_2030_df['Average_Discharge_Price_EUR_per_kWh'] * 100,
    marker='o',
    label='Regular Discharge Price'
)

plt.plot(
    annual_model_2021_2030_df['Year'],
    annual_model_2021_2030_df['Average_Charge_Price_EUR_per_kWh'] * 100,
    marker='o',
    label='Regular Charge Price'
)

plt.title('Discharge Price + Incentive per kWh for EV Owners')
plt.xlabel('Year')
plt.ylabel('Euro cent / kWh')
plt.grid(True)
plt.legend()

save_plot('discharge_price_incentives_yearly')
plt.show()

annual_model_2021_2030_df[['Year', 'Surplus_per_kWh_EUR_Cent']]

Year Surplus_per_kWh_EUR_Cent

4 2025 0.253175

Readings¶

  • How Vehicle-to-Grid and Smart Charging Influence EV Battery Aging https://cdn-website.mobilityhouse.com/25-10_WP_RWTH_EN_b135edc9631bdfda6acb27a816953fa9.pdf
  • Vehicle to Business: four uses cases https://scale-horizon.eu/use-cases/vehicle-to-business/
  • Analysis of the Feasibility of Integrating Electric Vehicles into Vehicle-to-Grid Systems in Hungary https://www.scientific.net/AST.165.235
  • Vehicle-to-grid feasibility: A techno-economic analysis of EV-based energy storage https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apenergy.2017.01.102
  • Bidirectional battery electric vehicle fleets in commercial applications: Exploring synergies and substitutions with stationary battery storage https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apenergy.2025.126884
  • Modeling and evaluating bidirectionally chargeable electric vehicles in the future European energy system https://doi.org/10.1016/j.egyr.2022.10.277
